title: Pronouns & Verb Agreement
situation: Master who does what — how pronouns control verb forms in Hindi

phrases.hindi: main / tu / tum / aap / woh / hum
phrases.english: I / you(intimate) / you(casual) / you(formal) / he-she-they / we
phrases.context: Hindi has 3 levels of 'you': tu (intimate), tum (friendly), aap (respectful). Using wrong one = social disaster
phrases.pronunciation: MAIN / TU / TUM / AAP / WOH / HUM

phrases.hindi: main karta hoon, tu karta hai, tum karte ho, aap karte hain
phrases.english: I do, you(int) do, you(cas) do, you(formal) do
phrases.context: Same verb 'karna' changes helper: hoon/hai/ho/hain. The verb ending (-ta/-te) also shifts for tum/aap
phrases.pronunciation: MAIN kar-TA hoon, TU kar-TA hai, TUM kar-TE ho, AAP kar-TE hain

phrases.hindi: mujhe / tujhe / tumhe / aapko / usko / humko
phrases.english: to me / to you(int) / to you(cas) / to you(formal) / to him-her / to us
phrases.context: Object forms — used when something happens TO someone. 'mujhe bhookh lagi' = I'm hungry (hunger struck to-me)
phrases.pronunciation: MUJH-e / TUJH-e / TUM-he / AAP-ko / US-ko / HUM-ko

phrases.hindi: mera / tera / tumhara / aapka / uska / hamara
phrases.english: my / your(int) / your(cas) / your(formal) / his-her / our
phrases.context: Possessives — these also change gender! mera (my-masc), meri (my-fem), mere (my-plural). Same pattern for all
phrases.pronunciation: ME-ra / TE-ra / tum-HA-ra / AAP-ka / US-ka / ha-MA-ra

phrases.hindi: meri behen / mera bhai / mere dost
phrases.english: my sister / my brother / my friends
phrases.context: Possessive matches the OWNED thing's gender: meri (fem noun), mera (masc noun), mere (plural noun)
phrases.pronunciation: ME-ri be-HEN / ME-ra BHAI / ME-re DOST

phrases.hindi: woh jaata hai / woh jaati hai / woh jaate hain
phrases.english: He goes / She goes / They go
phrases.context: 'woh' means he/she/it/they! Same word. Verb form tells you: jaata=he, jaati=she, jaate hain=they
phrases.pronunciation: WOH jaa-TA hai / WOH jaa-TI hai / WOH jaa-TE hain

phrases.hindi: aap baitho / tum baitho / tu baith
phrases.context: Commands change by pronoun: aap/tum get '-o' or '-iye' (polite), tu gets bare root. 'aap baithiye' = very polite
phrases.pronunciation: AAP BAITH-o / TUM BAITH-o / TU BAITH

phrases.hindi: mujhe lagta hai ki woh nahi aayega
phrases.english: I think (it seems to me) that he won't come
phrases.context: 'mujhe lagta hai' = it seems to me / I think. Literally 'to-me it-seems is'. Indirect construction — very natural in Hindi
phrases.pronunciation: MUJH-e LAG-ta hai ki WOH na-HI aa-YE-ga

phrases.english: I need to ask you something
phrases.context: 'tumse' = from you (tum + se). Pronoun + postposition forms: mujhse (from me), tumse (from you), usse (from him/her)

phrases.hindi: apna kaam karo, mera kaam mujhe karne do
phrases.english: Do your own work, let me do my work
phrases.context: 'apna' = one's own (reflexive). Used when subject and possessor are same person. 'Main apna kaam karta hoon' = I do my own work
phrases.pronunciation: AP-na KAAM ka-RO, ME-ra KAAM MUJH-e kar-NE do

grammar_notes: Three 'you' levels: tu (intimate — lovers, God, very close friends, anger), tum (casual — friends, peers, younger people), aap (formal — elders, strangers, respect). When in doubt, use 'aap'
Verb agreement pattern: main→hoon, tu/woh→hai, tum→ho, hum/aap/woh(plural)→hain. Plus verb ending: -ta with tu/woh, -te with tum/aap/hum
Possessives change for gender of owned noun: mera phone (my phone-masc), meri gaadi (my car-fem), mere kapde (my clothes-plural). Same rule for tera, uska, hamara etc.
'woh' is the Swiss Army knife pronoun: he, she, it, that, those, they. Only the verb form or context tells you which meaning is intended
Oblique case (before postpositions): main→mujh, tu→tujh, woh→us, hum→hum. Add -e/-ko for 'to': mujhe, tujhe. Add -se for 'from': mujhse, tumse
culture_notes: Using 'tu' with someone who expects 'aap' is deeply offensive — like calling your boss 'dude' x100. Start with 'aap', downgrade only when invited
'Apna' (one's own) is unique to Hindi/Urdu. It avoids ambiguity: 'usne apna kaam kiya' = he did HIS OWN work (not someone else's)
practice_prompt: Practice pronouns and verb agreement. Describe your family, tell me about your friends, or ask me questions using different 'you' forms. I'll catch any agreement mismatches and explain the rules.
